FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes data from the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, 5.1 data release, 21 U.S. research sites, baseline ages 9-10 years (N = 11,875 at baseline; analytic sample N = 2,294).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis applying connectome-based predictive modelling (CPM) with 10-fold cross-validation to resting-state fMRI data (baseline) and SMAQ scores (4-year follow-up).

FT_IC6.evidence: No indication this is a duplicate or preprint of an already-included peer-reviewed paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: SMAQ (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire) scores are the predicted outcome (DV); whole-brain resting-state functional connectivity (neural individual-trait variable) is the IV. CPM correlation r = 0.055 (p = 0.016) is a numeric estimate where the variable label is an SM-specific scale (SMAQ) paired with a neural/individual-trait-level predictor.

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative results reported: CPM Pearson r = 0.055, p = 0.016 (covariate-controlled model); permutation p = 0.02; uncorrected r = 0.066, p = 0.003.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis with numeric result: CPM produces predicted vs. observed SMAQ correlations with permutation-tested p-values.

FT_EC7_reason: All three FT-EC7 conditions are met: (a) longitudinal design — baseline rs-fMRI predicts SMAQ severity at 4-year follow-up; (b) SMAQ (SM) is the DV in every analysis in the paper — fMRI connectivity is always the predictor; (c) there is no analysis anywhere in the paper in which SM is the IV. No cross-sectional SM-as-DV analysis is present to trigger the carve-out.
notes: Paper is EXCLUDED under FT-EC7. The entire analysis predicts PUSM (SMAQ) from neural connectivity; SM/PUSM is never used as a predictor of any other outcome.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes two-year follow-up data from the U.S. ABCD Study (5.1 data release), 21 U.S. research sites, N = 10,973 total; analytic sample N = 1,036.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis applying CPM with 10-fold cross-validation to task-based and resting-state fMRI data and VGAQ scores.

FT_IC6.evidence: No indication this is a duplicate or preprint of an already-included peer-reviewed paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: The only behavioral measure analyzed is the VGAQ (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire), which assesses problematic gaming severity across single-player or online multiplayer video games with no specification of mobile/phone-based modality. Per binding clarification, gaming-only scales (VGAQ, problematic-gaming scales without explicit mobile/phone modality) fail IC2 unless the paper also separately measures a qualifying phone/SM variable with its own coefficient. No phone or SM variable appears anywhere in this paper.

notes: Excluded at FT-IC2 (FT-EC2). VGAQ measures gaming disorder without phone/SM modality specification; no qualifying phone or SM variable is measured separately anywhere in the paper.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes data from the U.S. ABCD Study (5.0 data release), 11,878 children recruited from 21 U.S. sites, baseline ages 9-10 years.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using linear mixed-effects models and mediation analysis on ABCD data.

FT_IC6.evidence: No indication this is a duplicate or preprint of an already-included peer-reviewed paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Section 2.2 describes per-device screen time including texting and video chat; Section 3.1 states 'We also examined the relationship between screen time on each digital device and each domain of cognitive function, and summarized the results in supporting information (Table S1 and Fig. S1).' Texting and video chat are phone/SM-specific items. The other side is cognitive function (individual-trait-level: working memory, processing speed, executive function, language, episodic memory). Per-device breakout estimates thus pair phone/SM-specific items with individual-trait outcomes.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple regression coefficients reported: e.g., executive function b = -0.212 (95% CI -0.301 to -0.122, p < .001, β = -0.051); processing speed b = -0.327 (p < .001, β = -0.042); mediation indirect effects also quantified (e.g., β = -0.015, 95% CI -0.028 to -0.005, p = .016 for putamen mediating screen time → executive function).

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analyses with numeric results: linear mixed-effects regression with p-values and CIs; bias-corrected bootstrap mediation with 10,000 samples.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional (authors explicitly state 'the cross-sectional design of the analysis limits our ability to infer a causal relationship'). FT-EC7 requires longitudinal design; cross-sectional design is sufficient to disqualify EC7.
notes: Paper is INCLUDED. Cross-sectional ABCD baseline analysis; screen time (with phone-specific per-device breakouts including texting and video chat in supplement) associated with cognitive function domains.
